evidence: Comments consistently explain WHY, not WHAT — e.g., 'NaN > X is always false — would bypass maxPerTip' (tip-spend-cap.ts:34), 'Process-invariant: container status cannot change during process lifetime' (connect.ts:277)
No restating comments observed — checked across toolkit, guards, pipeline, and plugins modules
toErrorMessage() utility extracted from 8+ inline definitions into errors.ts — consolidation of boilerplate
withToolWrapper eliminates cross-cutting boilerplate (timing, error wrapping, observer) rather than duplicating it per tool
observe() calls in action-executor are purposeful telemetry for reactive event processing, not nosy entry/exit logging
Step-numbered comments in pay.ts (9-step D402 protocol) and publish.ts (2-step pipeline) aid navigation through multi-step protocols
No pass-through wrappers found — wrappers like withToolWrapper and createPinnedFetch add behavior (timing, DNS pinning), not just forwarding
Generic names avoided — domain terms used consistently (attestDahr, publishHivePost, transferDem, resolvePostAuthor, checkAndRecordWrite)
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: session-runner.ts at 4144 lines has accumulated significant orchestration logic. Some observe() calls in action-executor.ts are paired with info()/warn() calls that slightly duplicate context. A few files have section-header comments (// -- Types --, // -- Constants --) that add vertical noise without aiding IDE navigation.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase shows minimal AI-generated debt. Comments explain constraints and rationale rather than restating code. Error handling is consolidated via shared utilities (toErrorMessage, withToolWrapper) rather than copy-pasted boilerplate. Domain terminology is used consistently over generic names. The observe() telemetry calls serve production monitoring needs. The only minor AI-style patterns are paired info()/observe() calls that slightly overlap in context, and section-header comment blocks that add some vertical noise.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: A developer working here would find their attention spent on logic rather than noise. Comments are purpose-driven, abstractions add real behavior, and domain terms are precise. The code has clearly been through cleanup and consolidation (toErrorMessage extraction from 8 files, withToolWrapper eliminating per-tool boilerplate). The minor paired logging and section headers keep this from exemplary but represent isolated rough edges, not a systemic pattern. Score around 89.

issues.summary: action-executor.ts pairs info()/warn() with observe() calls that partially duplicate context
evidence: Lines 153-166: ctx.info() logs the event, then ctx.observe() logs the same event with slightly more structured data
Lines 167-178: ctx.warn() logs a failure message, then ctx.observe() logs the same failure with structured data
12 observe() calls in the file, most paired with a preceding info()/warn() that conveys overlapping information
issues.suggestion: Consider having observe() also emit to the console log when appropriate, reducing the need for separate info()/warn() calls. Alternatively, accept this as intentional dual-channel logging (human-readable console + structured JSONL) and document the pattern.

add.description: Across toolkit, guards, and pipeline modules, comments consistently document constraints, invariants, and non-obvious decisions rather than restating code. Examples include NaN bypass prevention notes and process-invariant caching rationale.

toErrorMessage was extracted from 8+ inline definitions into errors.ts. withToolWrapper eliminates timing/error/observer boilerplate across all 9 toolkit tools. safeParse provides a single prototype-pollution-safe JSON parser.

add.header: Domain terms preferred over generic names
add.description: SDK bridge methods use domain-specific names (attestDahr, publishHivePost, transferDem, resolvePostAuthor) rather than generic verbs. Guard functions are named checkAndRecordXxx following a clear verb+noun pattern.

action-executor.ts uses both console logging (info/warn) and structured observation logging (observe) for each event action. The overlap is intentional — console is for human operators, JSONL is for machine analysis — but could be reduced.
